planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Drummond's onion is native to the limestone hills, rocky prairies, and open woodlands of Texas, Oklahoma, and New Mexico, where it blooms in March–May in dry, thin soils exposed to full sun. It tolerates the alkaline, calcareous soils of the Texas Hill Country and Gulf Coast prairies well. The bulbs go summer dormant after seed set.

directions: Plant small bulbs in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Drummond's onion is a native Texas wild onion — one of the most widespread and ecologically significant small bulbs of the central Texas prairies and limestone hills — producing in early spring (March–May) compact, rounded umbels of white to pink or rose-pink, six-petaled, star-shaped flowers on stems 6–12 inches tall. Each individual floret is small and star-shaped, the petals with a pink to rosy-pink midrib that gives the whole umbel a clean, pink-and-white color. It grows naturally on the limestone-based soils of the Edwards Plateau, Blackland Prairie, and Piney Woods margins — some of the most distinctive prairie landscapes of Texas — and serves as a native spring bulb groundcover that blooms when little else is flowering in the dry prairies. The bulbs and leaves are edible and mildly onion-flavored; Native Americans and early settlers used them as a food plant. It is a candidate for naturalizing in dry, rocky, sunny wild areas and native plant gardens in zones 5–9 throughout the central and southern prairie states. A small, rewarding native bulb for gardeners working with native prairie plantings in zone 9a.

common_name: Shining Spider Lily (Coastal Plain Spider Lily)
subcategory: coastal Southeast US native spider lily

planting.note: Plant bulbs with the neck at or just above the soil surface in moist to seasonally wet, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; shining spider lily is native to the flatwoods, wet prairies, and pond margins of the coastal plain from Virginia to Florida and west to Mississippi. It tolerates periodic flooding and requires consistently moist to wet soil during the growing season. The flowers are strongly fragrant in the evening.

directions: Plant bulbs in moist to wet, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; shining spider lily is a native coastal Southeast US Hymenocallis — growing naturally in the wet flatwoods, longleaf pine savannas, and seasonally flooded prairies and pond margins from Virginia to Florida and west along the Gulf Coast — that produces in summer the classic spider lily flower: a pure-white, fragrant, reflexed-petaled star with the distinctive central funnel-cup (the staminal cup or corona) and six long, narrow, spreading, white, ribbon-like outer perianth segments radiating outward from the cup. The flowers, 3–4 per scape, are sweetly fragrant in the evening, carried on sturdy stems above broad, strap-shaped, bright-green leaves. The species epithet 'rotata' (wheel-shaped) refers to the wide, flat, open rotation of the spreading perianth segments. An excellent native alternative to the more commonly grown tropical Hymenocallis species for moist or wet garden sites in the coastal Southeast, including rain gardens, pond margins, and consistently moist borders. The native ecological context — flatwoods, savannas, wet prairies — gives it outstanding adaptation to the natural seasonal wet-dry cycles of the Gulf Coast.

planting.note: Plant bulbs with the neck at or just above the soil surface in moist to seasonally wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Galveston spider lily is endemic to the upper Texas Gulf Coast and adjacent Louisiana coastal marshes and wet prairies, where it grows in the brackish to freshwater marsh edges and seasonally flooded grasslands. It tolerates brief salinity and periodic flooding. The common name 'Alligator Lily' reflects its preferred wet, marshy habitat.

directions: Plant bulbs in moist to seasonally wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Galveston spider lily is a Gulf Coast endemic — one of the few flowering plants whose native range is centered specifically on the Texas Gulf Coast and its immediate vicinity, making it a plant of genuine regional botanical significance for gardeners in the greater Houston-Galveston-Beaumont area. It blooms in summer, producing pure-white, fragrant, spider lily flowers in the classic Hymenocallis form — a central funnel-cup with six long, reflexed, strap-like white perianth segments spreading outward — adapted to the hot, humid, occasionally flooded conditions of the Texas coastal prairie and marsh edges. This is one of the most locally appropriate native plants for zone 9a Gulf Coast landscapes incorporating wet or rain-garden features, coastal prairie restoration, and native plant gardens in the Galveston Bay region. The common name 'Alligator Lily' records the marshy alligator habitat where it naturally occurs. Growing a Galveston spider lily in a Houston-area garden is a form of ecological stewardship for a plant restricted to a limited coastal range.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; crow poison is one of the most abundant native spring bulbs of the Texas Gulf Coast and southern Great Plains, blooming in February–April in lawns, roadsides, and disturbed areas. Despite being called 'crow poison' (Nothoscordum = 'false garlic'), it has no true toxicity; the name reflects the belief that it was toxic to crows. It self-seeds and multiplies freely.

directions: Plant small bulbs in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; crow poison is among the most familiar and abundant native spring bulbs of the Texas Gulf Coast, coastal prairies, and southern Great Plains — blooming reliably and prolifically in February through April in lawns, meadows, roadsides, and native areas, often producing large, naturalized sweeps of small, white, star-shaped flowers that are a characteristic sight of the Texas spring. Each individual flower is small, white, six-petaled, held in an open umbel of 6–12 flowers, similar in appearance to wild garlic but distinctly without the garlic-family smell (Nothoscordum = 'false garlic') — the leaves have no onion or garlic odor, which is the practical field characteristic distinguishing it from Allium species. It blooms early in the season, providing nectar for early emerging native bees and small butterflies. Despite the alarming common name 'crow poison', it has no significant documented toxicity to birds or mammals — the name appears to have been based on mistaken observation or transferred from another plant. An excellent, self-sustaining native bulb for lawn naturalizing and prairie garden settings in zones 6–10; one of the plants that makes Gulf Coast spring lawns worth not mowing until April.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4–5 inches deep in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gritty soil in full sun; Orphanides' tulip requires hot, dry summer dormancy to persist — suited to Mediterranean-climate gardens and dry, well-drained rock garden or raised bed conditions in hotter, drier zones. The Whittallii and Hageri forms (sometimes treated as varieties or separate species) have warm bronze, apricot, and orange-bronze color forms. It naturalizes well in lean, dry, sunny conditions.

directions: Plant bulbs in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Orphanides' tulip is a wild tulip species from the rocky, sun-exposed, dry hillsides and stony garigue of Greece and Turkey that occupies a distinctive niche among wild tulips: the flowers are warm-toned in bronze, apricot, terracotta, orange-bronze to brick-red with a pale or contrasting outer petal surface — colors that are rarely seen in tulips and difficult to describe precisely, varying through the population from burnt-orange to warm bronze to dark coppery-red, often with a contrasting buff, cream, or olive-yellow on the outer surface of the petals, creating a bicolor effect when open that shifts through several harmonious warm tones from bud to full bloom. The related Hageri form has darker, more saturated brick-red to terracotta flowers; the Whittallii form has vivid orange and is among the most orange-flowered of all tulips. All forms are excellent naturalizers in lean, very well-drained, hot, sunny positions where summer is dry — rock gardens, gravel gardens, raised beds with lean substrate — gradually building colonies of the distinctive warm-toned flowers that bridge the spring-to-early-summer gap in the tulip season.
